Welcome back everybody, how was the first session? Hope you guys had a good time. All right, so I'm excited for this next part. So we're digging deep into the actual perfect webinar. I almost hate that I called it the perfect webinar, but I was in the webinar mode and we did it because it's weird, especially since we're talking about stage selling as well. But it's the same thing, the concept's the same. Selling from stage and selling from webinars is very, very similar. The different nuances are when you're on stage, there's ways that I anchor the stage, there's ways where I push people around on the stage, there's ways you do stuff, and I'll show that during the presentation. But the most part of my presentation is almost the same. Again, a few little things we do that are different, live that I don't do on a webinar, but for the most part it's almost the same. So this is the perfect webinar script. Now I talk about the perfect webinar in the .com Secrets Book. That's the first time I first kind of shared it. In fact, the real story behind it is again, probably 12 years of my life doing a really bad stage pitch, tell us seminars that we talked about, then webinars and then live events and everything. And as I was doing the .com Secrets Book, this is the first time I started doodling out all these ideas and put them in a format. And about the time was when we launched Minor Circle Program and Liz Benny came in. And the time everyone was trying to do free plus shipping offers and stuff like that, and I was talking to Liz Benny, and she was like, what should I do? And I was like, you need to do a webinar. And I don't know why, but I felt like with her personality and her energy, I was like, this is the right vehicle for what you need to do, you need to do a webinar. And I'd never really taught webinars before. She's like, okay, well, cool, I'm gonna do it. What do I need to do? And I was like, let me get back to you. And so I spent the next three days, I don't know if she even knows this story, but I started going back to all my old webinars, all my notes, all my stuff, and just everything I've ever had, and I started looking at the patterns of what did I do consistently over and over and over again. And I started doodling it all out. I spent a couple of days doing this. And finally, I came out, I doodled out this little doodle. That was the original Perfect Webinar. Some of you guys have seen it. And in fact, it's in here somewhere. But anyway, I doodled out first picture out. And then I told her, I was like, hey, I'm gonna do a training tomorrow on Perfect Webinar, you should be on it. And so for all my little mastermind group of time, probably like 15 people or so, I did this training. It was an hour-long training. I just took the doodle and I walked through it. Like, this is what we did, how we did it. I kind of walked through the whole thing. And then people were like, oh. And for the last like three years, I've given that away for free, free play shipping was the DVD of that original presentation with the script. And we sold tens of thousands of them. And from that, it's insane how many people have actually launched Perfect Webinar from that. Dan Henry, you read the dot com secrets book, which had basically that doodle, that's it. And he launched the webinar from zero to a million dollars in like, I think four or five months. He was at the time the fastest person from start to the two comic club. Since then we've got tons more. And I can tell you hundreds of success stories of Perfect Webinar. When we did the expert secrets book, one of the big reasons why I wanted to do this is I wanted to go deeper on the webinar because it's the key to all sorts of mass selling. Even if you're not using the webinar, like again, that's why I wish I wouldn't call it Perfect Webinar, because it's also the perfect pitch for video sales there. It's a perfect pitch for an email sequence. It's a perfect pitch for product launch sequences. Any type of mass selling, this framework is for all types of mass selling. So even if you're like, or else I will never do a webinar ever, doesn't matter. If you're like, I'm never gonna speak from stage ever, doesn't matter. If you're trying to sell to the masses, meaning sending emails, videos, anything, this concept is the same. And I've seen people use it now in almost every market that I can dream of. I've seen people use it to sell chiropractic care. I've seen people use it to sell soap and lotions. I've seen people use it to sell supplements. I've seen people use it to sell pretty much everything. And so that's where I kind of prefaced no one discounts, it's don't be like, oh, this doesn't work for my business because I will strangle you. I will literally come to where you at and I will strangle you. And then I will show you me doing the perfect webinar on 10X in your company overnight. Like it just works, I promise you that. It works off the psychology of you're selling to the masses where I don't have the ability to ask to 101, tell everybody everything, I gotta do it at kind of a mass scale. Now a couple of things kind of lead up before I kind of go into this. One of the biggest things that I learned, I told you about this in the last session, like when I first started doing my first time speaking from stage, and the problem that a lot of us are gonna have, especially a lot of you guys, the sales people don't have as big a problem with this, but a lot of the educators who are selling information and products and things, you're gonna struggle with this because you're in this emotional dilemma where you think that the way you serve the audience the best is to teach them the step by step process of how to do what it is you do, okay? And that was my first big mistake. My first thing I went up there, I stood up on stage and I showed them here's how you do what I do. I showed in step one, step two, step three, I got into technical detail. I basically did a whole training course and then tried to slap a pitch at the end, and nothing crickets, okay? And I don't know about you guys, but I know that no matter how much I try to serve you in a step by step process, in a 60 minute presentation, I'm not gonna be able to give you all the tools you need. It's just impossible, right? And so you're not actually doing them a service. You're giving them a piece of the step by step and then you withhold everything else from them because you think you're actually serving them at the highest level. And the reality is you're not. The biggest thing you can do, and this is, if I can get nothing else through your mind besides this, the biggest thing you can do to serve somebody is not to teach them the step by step. There's tons of people that understand the step by step on how to do something. You can go to YouTube and you can find the step by step, right? The biggest thing you can do during your time with them on stage or on a webinar is to figure out what is keeping them back. We talked before about this big thing with the result up here, right? What is keeping them from getting that result that they want, okay? They have these internal false beliefs that are keeping them from success. That's it, that's what you have to understand. And so your job as an educator, as somebody trying to serve them, is to figure out what those false beliefs are. We're gonna destroy those false beliefs so that they're gone so they can actually move forward. We'll find when you do this that people actually, you'll see the people actually using and following through on what it is you sell them will dramatically increase by doing this. But you do that and then you make them a special offer how they can actually now learn the process or buy the product or whatever it is, okay? And so that's the big thing I wanna stress is you're not teaching here, step one, step two, step three, you are breaking false beliefs and you do that through story, okay? Now it's funny because I have to say, like in fact, I had some other day told me, like I did a perfect webinar and people kept telling me like, like when are you gonna get to the teaching? If you do this wrong, you'll hear people say that. If you do it right, I don't think I've ever heard someone come to one of my webinars or my stage presentations who didn't feel like they learned a ton, right? I'm teaching them the what they need to do. I tell them stories to break the false belief patterns around the what and then the product is the how you do it, if you do this right, what's interesting is that you'll get more people after it's coming to you like, in tears, like you changed my life, you broke my belief pattern. Like now I see the future, the future's clear, it's bright, I know exactly where I'm going and they have the vehicle they need to actually do it, right? And so I wanna just stress that I was like, you're not gonna be teaching step by step but this style of teaching, which is gonna be different for most of you guys, will serve them better, it'll do more and it's gonna feel weird for you at first, okay? Most people who have a webinar who doesn't work and they come back, it's not working, why not? And I'm like, I'm not even gonna watch it, my guess is you're teaching too much. They're like, oh yeah, I'm teaching too much. Like you have to get away from that teacher mode and it's tough for all of us, we wanna get like, I'm excited about right now, cause I'm in teacher mode, I get to teach you guys all the step by step, I love it. I would never do this on a webinar cause then you would never buy and you'd miss out 90% of what I could offer you, okay? And so I want you to understand that. So it's all about stories. So before we get into like, for every webinar, there's four core stories I'm gonna tell. There's four core stories stacking the clothes, like that's it, that's how I do my presentation, okay? Four stories and stacking the clothes. And so we'll break down those pieces, how they all work, how they tie together. That's what I understand is that the bulk of all the content are stories. And so to begin with, you have to actually understand how do you actually tell a story? The better you become at telling stories, the better you'll become at selling products, okay? You heard the old cliche like facts tell, stories sell. So true, okay? Stories are the key. Now I printed out two of the handouts from the expert's secret book, this is gonna make my process a little easier sharing the stuff with you. So if you have the expert's secret book, there's a section on the Pifty Bridge. These are the actual scripts I'm gonna be walking us through today to begin with. Now first, I wanna give you some backstory on this whole concept of the Pifty Bridge script and where it came from. And right now I'm drinking, I don't know if you guys see this, this is blue water. This is actually a blue shark from a product that I drink from a company called Proovit. And Proovit is a network marketing company. I am a co-owner in and I'm not doing this to pitch you on Proovit because there's a story that's tied into this. And so when the company first came out, I had worked with the owner, I think it's Brian, and a really cool dude, I worked with another project that kind of, we put a lot of effort into it, a lot of work and it just, some businesses don't work, right? And it failed, didn't make any money, lost a ton of money, lost six months of my life and it was really tough. And then a couple of years later, you're like, hey, I got this new thing, I wanna be part of it. I'm like, no, there's no way I'll ever be part of it again. He's like, come on, man, it's gonna be amazing. I'm like, no, I don't, no, no, no, no, no, I'm out. And he's kept trying to ask me and then one day he's like, well, at least try the product. I'm like, sure, man, send me the product. So like a week later, I get a FedEx envelope in the mail and I open the FedEx envelope and I open up and there's two white baggies of white powder with magic markers written on it, keto-os. And I'm like, what is like a drug or something, you know, you melt me through the mail. And I asked him like, what is this, man? And he's like, so I'm gonna probably try it. I'm like, I don't, like, what's in it? He's like, just trust me, it's awesome. Like, I don't feel super comfortable taking white packages of white powder in the mail and just consuming them in my body. And so I just, I sent him by like on the side and they were there for a couple of weeks. And then one day I was heading to church. I was actually late for church. I hadn't eaten in the breakfast the morning and I am a Mormon, so I go to Mormon church. It was like three hours long and there's meetings afterwards before. And it's like, I'm like, I need to eat something. And I was like, I looked over, I saw his magic bag of white powder. I'm like, ah, who cares? I dumped something, shook it up, drank it while I'm driving. First thing I'm like, this tastes really, really good. And then I get there and then all day long, I have insane amounts of energy and focus. I wouldn't get tired. Usually I fall asleep in church at least five or six times. I was like, alert the whole time. And I get home and I was like, dude, whatever that was, that was awesome. I want some more. And so then he starts like doing this thing that most of you will do. And he did to me and he started spewing out all the technical stuff about why this thing was so amazing. I was like, I don't know what you're talking about. It tastes like candy. I feel really good. I want some more of it. And so then he's like, well, maybe you can be on the board. I'm like, I don't want to be on the board. And he's like, well, if you're on the board, maybe you can look at my sales script. I'm writing and seeing. And I'm like, ah, so finally sends me the sales script. I'm laying in bed one night. And it's this huge long sales letter he wrote. And it's all jantful of all the technical, the science panel works and all these things. I'm reading it. And I was like, he's gonna bug me every day unless I just do this. So I was like, you know what? I'm just gonna do this. So I like deleted his sales letter and I sat down and I started thinking, because the product what it does is put your body in ketosis. And I knew about ketosis. I had a friend who explained me one time and I was trying to think, I was like, what is the reason? Like, what's the story that I know about ketosis? Like, why do I care about that? I'm thinking about this. I remember like a year and a half earlier, I was at a marketing event. And one of my friends named Aaron Lilly, I hadn't seen him in a while. And he comes in and he's like, he looks really good. And I was like, okay. And then we go to dinner that night. And I remember he ordered chicken with a side of butter. And he's eating dinner and he's like, got a chicken thing and he's dipping it in butter and eating it. And I was like, I just like totally start making fun of him because that's what I do. I kind of make him fun of him about it. And he's like, no man, this is the secret. Like you put your body in ketosis and it burns all these fats and all that. I'm just like, whatever. I've no idea you're talking about. And I'm like, explain to me. So he tries to explain it two or three times. And finally he's like, okay. Have you ever built a fire before? I'm like, obviously yes. He's like, okay. So, he's like, when you build a fire, you know, if you have a fire, it's going, you get like little logs and you throw it on. What happens? You get like little carbs. Like when you eat Cheerios in the morning, it's happening. You get like a little burst of energy and then you're like dead the rest of the day. That's what happens in a fire. He's like, but if you have a fire and then you put logs on it, what happens? I'm like logs burn longer. He's like, yeah, that's awesome. Logs are kind of like protein. And so it's like, when you eat protein, like it burns longer and energy is there. He's like, what happens if you take, if you take a coals and put them in the fire? And I'm like, well, that's really good. It burns like all night long. He's like, yeah, that's the key. Like coals are like fat. He's like, so what happens if you just took fat, like you took coals and like try light a master, it doesn't work, right? If you train your body to get into a state where it can actually burn the coals, he's like, but when he does, they burn all night long. You have better, it's a better source of energy. I'm like, yeah, he's like, why have trained my fats? I had no longer to do carbs or proteins. Most probably just eat fats now because I got my body trained in a way where the fats are burning. I had more energy, more focused. I'm not tired, like all these things. I was like, okay, cool. That makes, like it made sense to me, right? I'm sitting in their bed that night and I'm trying to think, how do I explain this concept of ketosis that's so confusing? And I was like, I'm gonna use that campfire story. And so I sat down and wrote this little script about, have you ever built a campfire before? And I talk about log or, you know, kindling logs and proteins, kind of like losing weight. And I tell this whole story, I write this script out, sent to Brian in the morning, he was like, dude, this is really good. I'm like, well, because this is a story that makes sense, that bridges this gap with people who understand it. And anyway, hands up, give me equity for the company exchange for making this video. And we get it produced. And it's this little video that all the distributors have on the phone. And they meet someone now, they go, the person, they push a plan, they say, watch this video, the person watches the video and they're like, oh, that's ketosis, and they sell in the product. So story bridges the gap and then they sell the product. And that company went from zero to, I think this year they're doing like 400, 500 million dollars, like in two or three years. Really really fast growing company. It was all based on a really simple story. Okay, so I'm kind of part of this Prove It thing. I'm not an active distributor or anything like that. I'm just excited by it. I love the product and I love the community. And so Brian asked me to speak on the event. I see all these guys who are in these t-shirts and the t-shirts say like ketones are greater than glucose and I see these guys right around, they're grabbing people and you see the people walking through the hotel lobby and they see these people coming to them and they're running away from them. So I get to the front, I turn around, I look out, I see all these people here, right? I see them all and they're so excited, so passionate. They all got on spandex and the t-shirts and like everything. They all got their water bottle and they're drinking it. You know, tons of energy and everything. I'm just like, oh man, these guys are pumped. I'm looking at them like, how can I show these guys? How can I, like what did he explain to them? And then it hit me and I was like, I know exactly when you explain these people. And so I got on a little bite, born at Juice Pitcher. Now I kind of laugh. So okay, all of you guys were a normal human being, right? And then you were going on your merry day way, things were happening else and something happened to you. Somebody introduced you to this product that you're passionate about, right? Something happened. Do you guys remember what it was? I think about it, what was the thing that happened? And then I'm like, everyone of you guys had an experience. That's why you're here. It's why you're like here. Something happened, you had an experience. Okay, and then during that experience, you went on this journey where you tried the product. Like for me, right? I tried the product, it tasted like candy. Tastes like candy, gave me energy. I was like, this is amazing. And so I went on this journey with them, right? Something happened, you know, on this journey. And then over here, what happened is because you were sold, because you had this emotional, great experience, you came over here and you started geeking out, right? So I'm gonna put glasses on here. This represents the geek out zone, right? So you start geeking out, you start learning everything back, you start learning technology. You learn about beta hydroxybrite, you start learning about ketosis and glucose and all these facts, right? This is all your logic, okay? And then what happens then is then you get so excited. You want to change the world, you want to change your business life, you're so excited. So you take this concept and all of a sudden you see this cute little prospect over here coming down, who's just a normal person like you used to be. And you see them, you're like, okay, I've got one shot. I've got to change this person's life. I need to explain to them exactly why they need this. And so you come over here and you pounce on, the first thing you do is you take all of this technical mumbo jumbo, we call it techno babble, techno babble. And the first thing you do, because you know you got a short window, you take this and you jam it down their throat, like do you understand that you're burning sugar instead of fats and like beta hydroxybrite, salt and this and this and ketosis and bubble and all this and you're jamming all this stuff down, they're all in them as quick as you can. You got one shot at getting them and this person gets scared to death and then they run away, okay? And that was, I saw this in the hallway over and over and over again. This is what you guys are doing because you're so geeked out, you're so in love with it. But how many of you guys got started in this company today because you're excited about beta hydroxybutyrate salts? The one guy in the back is just a hand. I'm like, are you scientists? He's like, yeah, okay, you're the only guy. Nobody else was out the reason, right? I said, for everybody else, there's some experience, something happened that got you excited. Well, you actually geeked out and cared about this, but you did not get sold because of this. Something happened to you. What was your story? It's one of your guys' story. And they all sitting there and someone raises their hand and I'm like, what was your story? Well, this had happened to me. I was struggling with my weight and I tried everything in the past. I've tried carbs, I've tried arrow, I tried this diet, this diet, this diet. And then my friends gave it to me, I tried it. It actually tasted good, it curbed my appetite and I lost X amount of pounds and I got super excited and that's why I did it. I said, then what happened? And he's like, then I started trying to figure out why and I started to geek down. I'm like, how many of you signed up? He's like, not that many. I was like, the problem is because you're jamming down this. Tell them the story. The story is the key. And if you do this correctly, if you tell them the story the right way, what'll happen is that people will go on the story with you, this journey with you and we'll call this the epiphany bridge because what was it that gave you the epiphany of your thing, right? What was that epiphany? You tell people about your epiphany, if you do it right, they will have the same epiphany you had and they'll geek out and figure out the logic on their own. You don't know where about that. But if you want them to buy, if you want them to move, if you want to create movement in somebody's life, you have to give them the same epiphany you had. And so this is the big secret. This is the key, you guys. And so for all of you guys, whatever it is you're selling, because you may have some products, services, cars, services, like everyone's got something different, right? What was it that got you excited about this thing? If I was, I keep using car examples, I know there's a lot of car people from the 10X event, but man, if it was me, I'd be thinking back, when was the first time I fell in love with cars? How old was it? Was it my dad, was it my grandpa, was it my mom? Like what was it like? Like what did it smell like? What did it feel like? What was the thing that I was just like, I want to be part of this. I had to love this. I love the thing. What was that story? And that would be the story I'd be telling to get people to come on this journey with you. Not about this thing gets, the car commercials are so annoying to me. They always drop all this techno babble. I was like, I'm an affluent buyer. I make a lot of money. I couldn't buy a lot of cars. And I don't understand a word they're saying on any car commercial, because they spat out techno babble. It's hilarious. It's like, if they came back and told me, this is why I created this car, because of this. I'd be like, oh my gosh, I want that car, right? It's the story that brings people in. And so for you guys, I want to think back. Think back back at the time when you were a normal everyday person. When was that for you? When you were a normal person. Now think about the thing that you were selling that you're geeked out about. What was that experience that you went through that gave you the epiphany that made you come over here? This is the key that unlocks everything, is understanding that. Understanding the story. So when I tell something about funnels, the last thing I do, thing I never do, is talk about conversion rates and upsells and downsells and all that kind of crap, right? I talk about my potato gun story. That's not my epiphany, I'm like, oh my gosh, a funnel is a secret. That's the difference, right? When I tell my, anyway, and you'll see when we go through the thing, I'm just telling stories the whole time. Epiphany bridge story after epiphany bridge story. There's four core stories to talk about and there's a lot of little sub ones we've done as well that I'll show you guys. But it's all these stories. This is the key to everything. And so it's you learning how to tell stories in a way that gets rid of this techno babble, okay? One of the things I talk about in Expert Secrets is like every time you come to a word that's like has even a little bit of techno babble, you have to stop, it's kind of like this. We call them kind of like bridges, right? Like in the campfire video that people use to improve it. I'm like, I talk about how they're gonna get in ketosis and they're gonna have ketones going through their blood. If you hear me say ketones going through your blood, what are you thinking? I'm thinking like keys and ringtones and like that doesn't make any sense. So so they say ketones, I like, I stop and said, it's kind of like having a million little motivational speakers running through your body, giving you tons of energy. I'm like, oh cool, that's what it's kind of like. So anytime I'm telling any story, any kind of like even mild techno babble whatsoever, I stop and say, it's kind of like this. Okay, I don't know how long we've been going in hour, two hours, however long. I've told you guys probably 20, 30 stories since we started this morning, right? Every time I hit a thing that's kind of confusing, I try to stop and say it's kind of like this. I try to relate to something you already understand. So I'm using these things. The next thing is really, really important. They're not just telling somebody about my epiphany. Okay, if I tell them the epiphany, they're like, oh cool, that makes sense, right? I mean you guys have ever, you have something cool happen to you and you want to tell somebody the experience, right? And you tell them the story, you're like, hey, this what happened, you tell them the whole thing, they're like, oh cool, and you're like, no, no, no, you don't understand, and then you retell it a different way, and they're like, no, no, I heard you say the first time, and you're like, if you just would have been there, then you would have experienced, right? I mean, if it was not before, how'd somebody do that? The key is you have to get them to experience it, okay? When I tell you my stories, think about earlier today, I told you guys the story about the first time I spoke on stage, I didn't just say, I spoke on stage and I bombed, right? I tried to take you into that experience so you could feel the pain of it, right? I was standing on stage, normally even speaking, like someone turns the music on, but I got done, nobody turned the music on. I was standing there, I felt like, oh, it's just so embarrassed, and so I kind of like awkwardly turned off and I walked off the side, and then I went to the back and the speakers were there, and like, man, they asked me how I did, and I didn't sell any of so embarrassing, and so I like, I snuck away, and I faked like I had to go to the bathroom, so I went to the bathroom, I went to the bathroom, went to the hotel room, I locked myself in the room, and I was so embarrassed, and I just watched movies all day long, and I ordered a Hagen-Dazs ice cream and a coconut shrimp, and that's how depressed I was, I didn't want to leave the room, I was so embarrassed. How many of you guys felt like that emotion, right? Like you were there, you felt, you were on stage with me when I bombed, you saw the audience, you saw the other speakers kind of tease me, you laid in bed with me and coconut ice cream, coconut shrimp, and Hagen-Dazs ice cream, too embarrassed to go back down, and I told that, if I did it right, you guys lived it with me, you experienced it, you felt it, right? So I'm telling stories, I'm not just like, that's what I realized, I needed to learn how to sell from stage, no, like, they're not gonna have the epiphany, they're just gonna, it's gonna like, oh no, I get it, that makes sense, but if I tell it in the right way, where they go through an emotional experience with you, then they have the epiphany with you, then it changes everything, okay? So as I show you guys this presentation, notice how I do that, like when I'm telling stories, I talk about how I feel, and this is hard for some of the non-filling people, we've been doing a lot of personality profile testing here in our office lately, and I'm someone who, I'm less of a thinker, more of a feeler, so for me it's easier, it comes more naturally, like I felt like this, and I felt this, a lot of you guys who are more like thinkers, it's gonna be more difficult for you, so you have to start thinking back, like, how did I feel? It's gonna be hard for some of you guys, but you talk about that, because as soon as I tell somebody how I feel, if they're listening, they're gonna feel it as well, human beings have this really cool thing called empathy, so I can be in a room with 9,000 people, explain how I'm feeling, and everyone in that room can feel similar feelings, okay, that's the power of this mass selling, so when I know that, you understand the pattern, you can amplify it, so I'm gonna tell a story, but before I give the big a-ha, the thing that they need, I need them to feel it, sorry, I've hit my microphone, I need them to feel it the same way I felt it, because if they're in the same experience, in the same state that I was in, then they're gonna have the same experience that I had, okay? I don't know how many of you guys study Tony Robbins stuff, Tony talked a lot about state control, right? And so a lot of times, when he's talking about state control for yourself, like, if I was in a room ask, how many of you guys have ever been Tony Robbins event? How many of you guys been Tony Robbins event, okay? If you go to UPW when you walk on fire, I mean, teach the law to cool stuff at the event, but the main one, the main thing he's talking about is how to get into state, right? People always ask me, how do you always seem so happy? Because before I pick up a phone, do you do Facebook live? I get into state. When I'm going home with kids, like I'm driving home, I'm tired, I'm worn out, I'm relaxed, and as soon as I get in the door, I get into state, so when I'm there, I'm present for my kids, and I show them this presentation, like, I'm tired. So you guys know I read, I spent 18 hours yesterday reading, I got to midnight past, I'm tired right now, but I show up here, I'm in state, right? You can control your state. And if you look at how Tony teaches state controls, there's only a couple things you got to do. Here's a little triad thing here. Number one, and the most important one, is physiology, you change your physiology, right? So if I'm tired from like this, like this is a really weird thing about depressed people. They stand really depressed. Like it's really hard to be depressed when you're standing like this, okay? It's just hard to try it. Makes me feel depressed, like, look at your physiology, you're gonna be like this. Yeah. Okay? If you shift physiology, it changes things fast. If physiology is the fastest way to change our state, it's like, how do I shift my physiologies? Can you energy? It's music, it's caffeine gives me physiology. There's different things I need to change my physiology to change my state. Okay, physiology is the symbolist to change. So that's number one, is if I'm telling someone their story, I need to change their physiology. So I understand that, what am I gonna say to change their physiology? Don't look for them to have, like we know people have rapport and empathy. If I built rapport correctly, which I've been talking about how I built rapport during the presentation, if I built rapport correctly, and then I start talking about how I feel, they're gonna have empathy with me and they're gonna feel the same way that I feel, okay? So physiology, I understand that if I need someone to get someone in the right state to have the same epiphany I had, I'm gonna control their physiology and do that by telling the story and talking about my physiology. If I do that, they're gonna match me, okay? So physiology, and there's two other things, but I'm not gonna talk about those because there's focus, there's meaning, there's language, stuff like that, but the biggest thing is like, physiology is the number one thing that controls state. So that's for yourself, it's also true for your audience. I need to control their state, okay? All right, so that's kind of the epiphany bridge. So that's setting up, so it's important to understand that this is the key is story, right? And so how do I tell a story in a way that gets people in the right state, gets their physiology in a way that I need it, and gives them this epiphany, okay? And so for me, I went on like a, I don't know, seven or eight year journey trying to figure this piece out. The expert seekers book, I take it down on that whole journey. I'm not gonna take you on the whole journey, but I worked with Michael Hague, who's one of the top Hollywood screenwriters and coaches in the world. He works with people like Will Smith, writing the screenplays for movies and stuff like that. And I studied with him, we have him speak some of our events, trying to understand story from a process standpoint. If you look at almost every successful movie play in the history of all time, they all follow the same patterns called the Heroes Two Journeys. I talk about a diagram and break down the book, and so when you understand that, like here's, we know what story structure, we know what needs to look like, we know what human beings respond to based on, however many thousands of years human beings have been on this planet, we know what works. So we have to understand like, how do I put my story into the Heroes Two Journey into that process? And for me it was hard, it took me a while, finally I was able to break it down into a script, we call it the Epiphany Bridge Script, which we have right here in Milwaukee, I said, because this Epiphany Bridge Script is what is the way that I tell my stories to try to get the epiphany that we need, okay? So I'm gonna do this, I'm gonna erase this right now. I'm gonna go through these two doodles with you guys, but if you wanna see these closer, once again they're in the Expert Secrets book. So here you go, I'm gonna kinda go through these. So this is the Epiphany Bridge Script, and you're gonna notice me, when we watch the actual presentation, you'll notice me doing this over and over again. Some of these, like the nice thing about Epiphany Bridge Script is I can tell a story, and I can do this in a two or three hour long session, like a movie, or I can do an Epiphany Bridge Script in like two minutes, okay? And you'll notice me doing different variations of it as I go through here. Some I leave out a lot of details, some I put more details in, a lot of it depends on time. That's just nice, if I'm speaking in an event, I'm like, I've got 90 minutes, I know about how long my stories need to go. If I've got 30 minutes, I can tell the same stories if I pull all the details out, and I can still get the same job done, right? Okay, so here's the Epiphany Bridge Script, and these are the questions that kind of go along with it, and so these are the questions if you're like, how do I do this? Just grab these questions and ask these questions and you answer them, and it'll help you understand how to take someone through the process, okay? All right, so very beginning. Every good story starts with the backstory, because the biggest thing is you have to build rapport with your audience, or they're not gonna care about you, and they're not gonna listen to you, right? Like rapport is huge, and I'm gonna show you guys when we do the presentation, all the stuff I do to try to get rapport quickly with an audience, okay? If you have rapport, again, then it's easier to affect their physiology and get them in the right state and all those kind of things, so trying to always seem to get rapport quickly, and so to get rapport, people, is I have to get them to like me, right? And so part of that is like, and again, I talked about this in the last session, like what most people do to try to get people to like them is they like to brag about themselves. Like I was on CNN and Fox, and my best friend was President Trump, and I go golfing every day with this guy, and they always brag about this stuff, and I watched presenters do this, and I'm like, they're posturing trying to build rapport, and all they do by posturing is they're pushing people away. Okay, if you look at my posture, my postures are like very short, like glimpses, like oh, I did this by the way, and I shift back, right? I don't spend time there because I want people to understand why they should listen to me, but I don't wanna stay there because that's gonna, it causes distance, it doesn't bring people together, right? So what brings people together is the backstories. So I might say like, hey guys, I'm excited to be here today, I'm gonna walk you through, I made $3 million in 90 minutes, but to begin with, I'm gonna tell you this story, I'm gonna go to my story of me eating coconut shrimp because I failed, right? Because that's what builds your rapport, that's why people care about me because it's like, oh man, he was just like me, right? So we start on the backstory, if you look at any movie, all good movies start with the backstory, okay? And then the goal of the backstory is to build rapport with the hero who's going on this journey, right? We go to the Shire in Lord of the Rings because we have to build a relationship with the Hobbit, right, with Frodo. You go to Lightning McQueen, starts the show, starts cars, like on the raceway, in this initial race because it builds your rapport and you start building a relationship with the audience, okay? Now in the backstory, and if you look at, it's funny, all Hollywood screenplays, it's like the backstory happens from like page this page, this page, this page is when the hero always leaves, leaves the spot, right? It's crazy how like every movie follows the same thing, every movie you watch, look, and you'll start seeing this pattern. So I started story in the backstory, and in the backstory I wanna do a couple things, I wanna talk to people about my desires, okay? I'm talking about two types of desires, the external desire and then my internal desire. Now most people who tell stories are willing to share their external desire, like I wanna make a million dollars, I want it to lose weight, okay? If you wanna be a great storyteller, you have to become vulnerable, and this is tough for most of us, it's tough for me, still tough for me, but this is what gets people to build rapport with you, okay? If you say I wanna make a million dollars, they don't really care, it's like oh cool, but I need to talk about it, because that's the journey we're going on, is to do this and it's usually their same goal, but if you come back and now you share your internal desires, but the reason why I really wanna make a million bucks, not so much like care about a million dollars, but as I was growing up, my dad, blah, blah, blah, I wanted his respect, whatever, you tell the real story, why do you actually want a million dollars, and why? And they keep asking the question why, why, why, I'm like this is the real reason why I wanted to do it, okay? If you're willing to get vulnerable and explain the internal reasons why you did something, what's happening with most people is most people are willing to talk about their external desires. So when you're talking about the people listening in the audience, they're like oh yeah, I want that too, but as soon as you open up, you start telling your internal desires, and I just wanted the respect of my wife or my kids, or I wanted to feel good, like I just wanted that feeling when I come out that I like. And you share that? What happens is that that's the real reason why they want the thing too, no one cares about making a million bucks, nobody does. Why do they do it? They want a feeling, Tony Robbins at Last Fun Life and Live Events, so that all this we do stuff could want a feeling, right? The reason why someone smokes a cigarette they want a feeling, the reason why people fall in love is they want a feeling, there's a reason why people read a book because they want a feeling, the reason why you're here today is because you're hoping to get a feeling, like all of our life's based on feelings, and so when we're willing to share our real feelings, other people will be like, that's my feeling too. Oh my gosh, this person understands me, I'm just like them. So when you're willing to open up the internal, that's when you build connection, okay? Now if you leave the backstory before you have connection, you're gonna be in trouble. So it happens, if you've watched a movie and the end of it people died and you're like, eh, it's because they didn't spend a time in the backstory. I was watching, I can't remember what the movie was, the Venice Quaid movie with the volcanoes and everything, I remember the backstory happened, they left, and I didn't have any rapport with the hero, and I remember at the end he died and I was just like, man, I wish we had died a long time ago, that was really boring, you know? But if you have connection with the character in the backstory, then you care about this journey, okay? So the backstory happened, the tell backstory, I'm talking about my thing, I wanna try to do, but I share my internal desires, what I'm really looking for, and then I was in some vehicle, I was trying to get that thing through this thing, right? Notice the vehicle we talked about last session, like they're in the vehicle as well, trying to get a result, you were in a vehicle that didn't work, what was the vehicle you were in to try to get the result you're trying to share with them right now? There's something you were trying, most of you guys wasn't in the first shot, that all of a sudden like, I decided I wanted to sell stuff online, and then I made a funnel and made a million bucks, it's like, no, I tried eBay, I tried Shopify, I tried Crazils, I tried, da-da-da, what was the vehicle, or vehicles you try that you failed at, okay? Because this is this wall you're getting this journey, because of that, you had to leave, you couldn't stay in the shire, you had to leave the shire to go to Mordor to destroy this ring, you had to leave the Piston Cup race, to go to California to go race, to have the actual victory, to actually win, right? You had to leave the physical occasion, all heroes after the first, like 20% of the first script, they leave their hometown, they leave wherever the rapport is built with the audience, right, and they leave. So for you, you got stuck, all these vehicles you were trying to get the result, and then you had to leave, and you hit this wall, and it started you on this journey. And so this journey, you start going on this process, and somewhere in this process, this is where you have this epiphany, or an idea, or you met somebody, some people that break down the heroes to your journey, they talk about, there's always like a mentor, you met a mentor, you met, you had an idea, whatever it is, but something happened, we're also, you had this idea, it was like, oh my gosh, I need to get my body in ketosis, oh my gosh, like a funnel is the thing, oh my gosh, it's whatever your big aha moment was, right? And so you share it, here's the epiphany I had, and that epiphany opens up, boom, this new opportunity. I think this is the way I need to go. This is the thing that I need to do. And so then you go and you make this plan, okay, I'm gonna try this, and we're gonna go on this journey, and you start going down this path. Now the biggest thing about story to understand is that, story without conflict, nobody cares about. It's the conflict in a story, it's the, what's the word I'm looking for? It's the contrast that makes things interesting, right? If you're a designer, if you're a copywriter for anything, like the contrast of what makes things interesting. I was fat and I wanted to get skinny, I was happy, I was sad, like in the better the contrast, the better the emotion of the story is. And so during this plan of me, like here's my epiphany, I think this is the thing, and you start going on this journey, most of us will wanna talk about our success, like I figured it out, I'm amazing, no, like, tell the conflict, tell the struggles, it makes you real, it makes the story interesting, it's why people actually care about you. If you don't have the conflict, nobody cares, okay? Natalie Hudson, she spoke a fun-lacking lie about vulnerability, and she shared something that was really powerful, she said, if Russell came to all of us, instead he made $3 million in 90 minutes, we'd all think he was this arrogant, annoying person, right? Like, I hate that guy. But because, she's like, because all of us are listening to his podcast, and watching his videos, and heard the ups and the downs, the company crashing, going bankrupts almost twice, and all this stuff, that when, because he shared all the failures, when he won, we all celebrated together. Okay, for Natalie, the same thing for her business, like, because she shared the downs, when she had the success, everyone celebrated together. If you just shared the success, nobody would celebrate with you, and you're gonna lose them in the story, okay? So be willing to be vulnerable, and share the conflicts that happened during your, you're trying to execute this plan of the epiphany. I tried this, like, if I was trying to sell this, I wouldn't just talk about this ketosis thing, I wouldn't talk about, like, how it went off, and I started doing it, and guess what? I kept drinking this stuff, it tastes really good, but I'm still fat a year later, why? Because, like, it's hard, I couldn't give up this, and the problems, the mental struggle in my family, like, whatever, I tell the conflicts, and people are like, man, like, I'm in that same boat, I understand that. And then we do have the achievement, they also seed together, okay? Now the achievement over here, this is the, the fulfillment of your ex-tron's there, I wanna make a million bucks, I wanna lose weight, I wanted the new car, I wanted the whatever, right? And some movies, people don't ever hit the achievement, that's okay, Lightning McQueen didn't win the Piston Cup on episode number one, Rocky Balboa did not beat Apollo Creed in number one. You know, like, we don't always hit our achievement, okay? But the goal of the story is that the internal desires, the things that you're actually wanting, that's the journey of transformation. So the journey of achievement, and the journey of transformation. And so you can talk about, like, yeah, and then we made $3 million, 90 minutes, like, that's awesome, right? There's the journey of achievement, but the coolest thing is that when I got back home, finally, and then this isn't my story, like, finally my wife realized that whatever, my husband, or my dad, or finally, like, I was able to, like, blah, like, what was the transformation that you went on, that had success? The internal desire. And that's what makes these stories amazing, okay? So we start with the backstory, we talk about our external desires, we talk about our internal fears, and all that kind of stuff that came up. All the vehicles we tried to get at spot, and then boom, we hit this wall, and we had, no longer was a shit, it was a must, I decided I had to go on this journey, and went to try to figure this out, I left my job, quit my job, I, blah, blah, blah, whatever happened, something like whatever that moment was for you, you actually made the shift, and then the epiphany you had, created this new opportunity, the plan you went through it, the ups and downs of the plan, eventually the achievement, but then most importantly, the transformation. That is the story, okay? This is magic, like this, if you guys have nothing else to just tell a story, like this, and have a really good offer, then you'll make money. Okay, now the perfect webinar, I'm gonna walk you through, like, the amplification of the model, but if I just need to sell something, I'm gonna tell someone a really good story, I'm making a really good offer, okay? And those two pieces are enough to make most of you guys millionaires. Okay, now if you wanna get big, and 10X and all that kind of stuff, that's why, that's when you're gonna like, dig deeper into the other kind of things, but that's really the key, guys. You tell someone a really good epiphany bridge story, and they make them a special offer that gives them the opportunity to follow you with this new opportunity, that's enough to sell almost anything, okay? I do that all the time through emails, through videos. If I have a shorter format, I'm gonna do a epiphany bridge script and an offer, that's it. In fact, I did a whole training yesterday, hook, story, and offer. Hook, story, offer, that's it. Like, that's really it, okay? That's as simple as it can be. Now we're gonna complex it up a little bit, in a webinar or stage presentation, I've got 90 minutes, so I'm gonna do a little more complex, and because of that, I can do a lot more things and increase my conversion rates, but I'm gonna walk you through that. Now, if you get stuck with this, like, I had this printed out on my desk, it's sitting there, so I'm telling stories, I'm referencing it, like, oh, and then, I'm gonna start my internals, and I'm talking about my internals, and then I was going through this journey, and I hit this wall, and then it, and that's when I had the epiphany. Now, you tell that to the conflicts I had, like, I was trying this and this, and I use this all the time, so print this out, have it on your desk, and just sitting there and you're telling stories, you're doing Facebook lives, you're doing a blog post, and emails, like, have it there, and then these are the questions if you get stuck, it's like, number one, what was your backstory, it gives us, it gives us the vested interest in your journey? What was you wanting to accomplish? What was your external struggle with doing? What was your internal struggle? What was the wall or the problem here within your current opportunity that started you on this journey? What was the epiphany you experienced and the new opportunity that you discovered? What was the plan you created to achieve that you desired? What conflicted you experienced along the way? What was the end result you achieved? How was the transformation that you experienced? Okay, boom. Master this, use it over and over and over and over and over again. I probably used at least 30 or 50 bridges on you since we got started today, at least, maybe more, okay? Because I've mastered it, I do it subconsciously all the time. The other thing, I wasn't gonna share this, but when you're getting success stories from your students, don't be like, hey, can you send me a success story? Interview them, ask them, have a question for you. Like, tell them what's the reason why you got started on this journey, and they'll tell you. Like, well, what were you trying to achieve? I know you're trying to make money, but what was the internal desire? What did you really want? And they'll tell you these things, and they can make amazing emotional testimonial videos like that. In fact, Kaylin Poland, she did a really cool thing. She told me, if you don't know Kaylin yet, she's awesome, she's younger, she's got tattoos and nose rings and stuff like that. She's like, I know that there's an audience that I appeal to. She's like, I also know there's an audience that I don't. She's like, moms with a lot of kids, all kinds of things won't really relate to me. So she's like, so I went through and I found a bunch of moms in my Ladyboss community who are having success, and I had them, I said, I want to interview you live, we'd be willing, and they're all said yes. And so she made like a woofoo form, and basically had them answer all these questions, right? And so the person that sat down and spent an hour thinking through, like writing out all their questions to this Epiphany bird script, then she got these people on Facebook live, and she read it ahead of time, so she knew where she was going. She knew where the story was going, and then she entered, and she said, hey, so a lot of people in the Ladyboss community, like, they look at me and they're like, this is cool for you Kaylin, but I'm a mom, I've got kids, and so I want to bring you, like, tell us the backstory, how did you get involved with Ladyboss? And then boom, the person tells the backstory, and then what's cool about that too, a lot of times people, it's their first time on a Facebook live, they get really nervous or awkward, so Kaylin knew the story ahead of time, so she was like, well, tell me about this part, tell me about this part, she can like draw out the emotion of the story, so that's kind of a little hack, a little trick that's just super, super powerful. So this is the framework of a story, right? And man, I would tell for you guys, like, I would be practicing stories a lot. If you look at my day, I wake up in the morning, I see my kids, I try to tell them all a story every single morning, just practicing my storytelling skills. After that, I jump in my car, and you have my phone, and I do a podcast, where I usually tell a story. And at the office, and I usually tell the same story, it's like three or four people in the office, so I've told this story four or five times, usually before I ever publish a live on a webinar or a thing like that, right? Because I'm practicing it over and over and over again, you see what people respond to it, they don't respond to it, what am I getting boring at, what's good, what's hooking up, and I just, I practice telling stories a lot. And so if anyone wonders, like, Russ, why are you so good at this? I'm like, well, I've been telling these stories for a long time, but I have a whole inventory, and this isn't outside of this training, but I have a lot of our clients, I'm like, make a list of all your stories, and build an inventory of all these stories, because you're gonna need them all the time. When someone's got a concern, you gotta resolve it with story, and so it's like, the more you do this, the more you do it, the better you become at it. So make an inventory of stories, here's all my different stories, and again, I wish, make sure you read the book. We talk about inventory stories, how to create, and how to find the right stories, and how to use it to resolve concerns. I wish I could go deeper, but if we do, it'd be like 40 hours. So, all right, so we're talking about that. So the Pifty Bridge is kind of like bridges. That's kind of the overarching idea of an epiphany bridge, the story. And so again, you guys can go deeper here, inside the book if you want to, but the biggest thing is becoming good at telling stories, because the entire perfect webinar framework is literally, it's four stories, stacking and closing to the offer. So I'm gonna kind of map it out. And it's been a while since I've taught this news, so I'm kind of excited. I'm mapping it out, because as we go into the actual presentation, you're gonna see it, I want you to be like, oh, that's what he did here. Here's what he did here, here's what he did here, okay? So if you look at, making sure this gets on the camera, all right. So this is like, the beginning. This is Russell's doodles, hopefully you guys don't mind. All right, so this is an initial framework. Now I internally what we kind of call this, we call this like the one thing, the one thing, and then the three secrets, three. Okay, one thing I want to talk about, or just kind of say before you're too deep in this, it's funny is I've done this so much, I've taught some people on this, like when I do these presentations nowadays, it's hard because people are like, Russell's here, here it comes, here's his one thing, here's his three secrets. And so what's fun is that, fun like you live this year, we were doing our pitch for our Two Column Club X coaching program. I was like, if I start with like, my existing framework, as they know, everyone would have been like, here comes the pitch. And so we did the exact same framework, but we just, it's funny, if you guys go back and watch that, the same framework, but we tweaked it, and nobody knew it was a perfect webinar until like halfway through the offer, people were like, here's to the perfect webinar, and so we didn't even know. You can do it stealth, or you can do it the way that we kind of do it, it's the same thing, okay? So the one thing, so if you come back to what we talked about earlier, right? Opportunity switch, like I'm trying to give these everybody an opportunity switch, okay? So I was thinking like, what's the one thing I have to get them to believe if they're gonna take this new opportunity, right? And so that's kind of the first thing to think about. Now, when I'm starting this presentation, there's a couple things I do, you'll notice, I'm trying to build rapport quickly. I'm trying to get them to make small commitments fast, and if I use social commitments, it's even better. So commitments rapport, trying to build a relationship with attractive character, which is me, or you if it's your presentation, so by that. So I'm trying to like do some of the things at the very beginning, but at the same time, this is what I'm telling my very first at Pifty Bridge story, and this is what I call my origin story. Okay, it's the origin story about why I believe that this opportunity switch is what I'm taking to. So I'm basically selling somebody in the first 15 minutes of the webinar. I'm doing my first Pifty Bridge, and for some people, it's enough they would just go by right then. Like if I only have like something like, you have 20 minutes to sell something, I'm like, okay, I'm just gonna tell my origin story, and then transition to stack in the clothes, right? Cause that'll get a lot of people to go and buy. But then, because I've got 90 minutes traditionally, 60, 90 minutes, I'm usually, I go deeper here, and there's three more stories we tell, but they all have the same goal. The whole goal of this is to basically knock down the big domino, like what's the one thing I need them to believe? So I went to 10X, I was like, okay, the whole theme of this event is that we're gonna 10X your company, right? So my goal is like, if I can get them to believe that the only way that they're actually gonna 10X their company in the next 12 months is if they master funnels, and they have to give me money, right? That's my blue ocean, I'm the funnel dude, there's nobody else. If they wanna be successful, they wanna 10X their business, I'm the only way to do this. I have to get them to believe that, right? So I'm just like, oh, funnels are nice. If they leave like, oh yeah, funnels are nice, they're not gonna buy. I have to get them to believe that the only way to get the result that they want, so I know at this event they're trying to 10X their business, right? All you guys who are there, that was your goal, I need to 10X my business. So the only way you can 10X your business realistically in the next 12 months is by mastering funnels, that's it. If I get you to believe that, then you have to give me money, right? You have to, there's no if and or buts about it. If that's really your goal, and that's really the result you're looking for, and if I need you to believe that this is the only way to do it, then you have to give me money. Does that make sense? So for you, that's the argument you gotta make, it's like, what's the goal they have? I need to get them to believe that my blue ocean is the only way they can actually get that, okay? Now, if you didn't create your offer right, if you create an improvement offer, it makes this first part really, really hard, okay? Actually like, yeah, this is a better way to do it. It's like, yeah, but it's still the same way, there's a lot of ways, there's a lot of mousetraps. If I want this to be, I wanna be boldness. I want, you'll see, I'm pretty bold. The only way, my belief is the only way you can do this is by giving me money, that's it. In fact, I even do the price marinated, which I'm gonna show you guys, where I give them all a commitment. If I can prove to you that this is the way to do it, we give me 11,000 bucks, and everyone in the audience is like, yes, I will give you 11,000 bucks, you prove me that's what it is, okay? So the first thing about Origin Story, to try to explain why I believe that this new opportunity I'm giving them is the only way for them to get the result of the one thing that they actually are there for, right? So I'm telling my first Piffney Bridge story, right? So this is the Origin Story, so this is you telling your backstory. And the cool thing about this is it's doing, it's telling this first thing, it's your first shot at kind of knocking down this big domino, but I'm also, because I'm telling my backstory, I'm building a rapport, I'm doing all those other things, I'm building a relationship with attractive character, I actually care about me, and then it makes everything else easier as well, okay? So it's not just like I'm telling a story to brag, I'm telling a story because this is my first shot at trying to knock down that big domino, okay? Hopefully that makes sense. So that's the first story. So for each of you guys, think back, what is your Piffney Bridge story about? Why you believe, if I bring this back out, why you believe that this new opportunity that you're offering them is the secret to their success, why it's the only way they can actually get the result that they are trying to achieve, okay? There's an Origin Story. Anything else? Yeah, all right, have my notes here. The Origin Story is how you made that opportunity switch, here's all the other vehicles I tried in the past, but I found out funnels were the key, and I'm gonna convince you today that this is the key, and if I convince you that, boom, like it's the only way to do it, then you gotta give me money, okay? All right, so that's kind of the Origin Story. Your story about how you made that opportunity switch, how you shifted from all the vehicles you tried in the past, and figure out this was the key, this what gave you the achievement, and now you're gonna show them how the same thing is true for them, okay? So I'm gonna slow them down, so I'm making sure I'm not missing anything as I move from point to point. All right, now one thing I'm gonna talk about here, this is a concept called the Price Marinate that I learned from Myron Golden, and I'd never done it before, I'd done it on a webinar once, but I did it incorrectly, so this is the first time I'd ever done it, and I was so scared to do this. I almost took it out of my sleds the day before, finally I got all, I'm just gonna do it. So the concept of Price Marinate, so Myron taught me this, he said that he does these like three or four day long events, and the very first day he comes in and he's like, talking about his high-end programs he sells, and he'll get a marker on the whiteboard, and he'll put it on the corner, and he'll be like, my high-end program is $39,000 a year, and right up there, and people see it, and the first thing that happens, they get a sticker shot, like sticker shock, of like, holy crap, $39,000, that is a lot of money, and he leaves it there, and that number's there for the entire three-day event, he's like, my goal now for the next three days is to convince them that whatever I'm selling is worth at least $39,000, right? So he's doing other things, he's showing this, showing this, showing all these people making money, and showing all these different things, and by the end of day three, that number starts getting like, it starts getting cheaper in their mind, it gets cheaper and cheaper and cheaper and cheaper, to eventually it's like, man, $39,000 would be worth it, then I could do, you know, then if it's gonna make me a million dollars, $39,000 is really, really cheap, right? So that price, you take the sticker shock off, you marinate, marinate, marinate it, and then when you come back to the actual offer, then it's like, actually, for you guys, it's not gonna be $39,000, you get it for $10,000, they're like, I've been trying to convince my, I've been trying to figure out for the last four days, three days, how to pay $39,000, now I know it's only 10? Oh, I'm in, it makes it really, really easy. So I wanna do that here, I wanted to make it where, I wanted everyone to believe that to, whatever it is I'm gonna sell at the end, it's gonna cost $11,552. So what I do, $550, that's how I do the price, Mary, those who are 10 X, you remember this, I told my original origins for about funnels, and I said, I believe funnels are the only way that you can 10 X your business, in fact, if I can prove to you guys today that funnels are the only way, right, my one thing, the only way you can 10 X your business in the next 12 months, if I can prove you that, will you give me 11,000 bucks? Because if I give you, because that's basically what you mean, you made a million dollars. So if I made you, if I, you know, whatever, if you're 10, if you're 100,000, I can't remember exactly what I did, we'll see it together, it's been a little while, but like, I basically showed like, if you 10 X your business was that, that is that extra 100,000, is it a million, is it 10 million, so if I can give you an extra 10 million dollars in your business to give me 11,000 dollars, and everyone's like, yeah, it's okay, cool, because in about 45 minutes from now I'm gonna ask you guys, I'm gonna ask you to pay me 11,550 dollars to help you to do this, so I just want you to be prepared for that. So I did the price marinade, and so what happens, I'm ripping off the sticker shock, everyone's like, 11,550 dollars, holy crap, and then I'm convincing, through the whole rest of this process, I'm convincing, by the time I get the end, the mind of it, Kevin, I pay 11,000 bucks, when I come back, it's only three, boom, now it seems super inexpensive, right? So that's kind of the concept. So Origin Story happens there, I did the price marinade, again, the first time I ever done it, I loved it, I will probably do it again. Anyway, feel free to try it or not try it, it's intimidating, but man, when it works, it works really good, okay, all right. So again, tell Origin Story, tell me every first story, again, a big percentage of the audience, that's all they need, and they will give you money right then, because I can do the transition to my offer and my clothes right now, but because we've got the format where we're on stage for, usually traditionally, the way the time they work, so you've got 60 minutes on stage, and then 30 minutes for your pitch, okay, so it's a 90 minute presentation.
